# (PART) LEARNING PARADIGMS {-}

The classic split of supervised, unsupervised, and reinforcement learning is only the first cut. A wider family of paradigms describes how a model gets its training signal, how little data it can learn from, where it runs, and how its objective is structured. This part groups them into four families: data-availability paradigms (semi-supervised, self-supervised, active, and weakly supervised learning); sample-efficiency paradigms (few-shot and zero-shot, meta-learning, transfer learning); operational and streamed paradigms (online, federated, ensemble, continual learning); and architecture or goal-driven paradigms (curriculum, imitation and inverse reinforcement learning, transductive, adversarial, and metric learning).
